name: Business Law
description: A survey of the US legal system and its application to business, covering contracts, business organizations, employment law, intellectual property, consumer protection, torts, criminal law, and international and ethical dimensions of business law.

modules.title: Introduction to the US Legal System
modules.objectives: Distinguish civil from criminal law; identify the sources of law (constitutional, statutory, common, administrative); understand the structure and jurisdiction of federal and state courts.
modules.readingNote: OER Business Law — Ch 1: Introduction to Law and Legal Systems
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The US Legal System Explained
modules.practiceNote: OER Business Law — Sources and Types of Law Review
modules.assignment: Create a diagram of the federal court system showing all levels from district courts to the Supreme Court; for each level, indicate its jurisdiction and give a real-world example of the type of case it would handle.

modules.title: Sources of Law & Court Structure
modules.objectives: Explain the hierarchy of laws (constitutional supremacy); trace a case through the appellate process; understand the doctrine of stare decisis and the role of precedent in common law.
modules.readingNote: OER Business Law — Ch 1.3: The Court System
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Checks, Balances, and the Courts
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How a Case Moves Through the Courts
modules.practiceNote: OER Business Law — Court System Review
modules.assignment: Research a famous Supreme Court case that affected business (e.g., Citizens United, Hobby Lobby); summarize the legal issue, describe the Court's decision, and explain how it changed the business legal landscape.

modules.title: Contract Law: Formation
modules.objectives: Identify the essential elements of a valid contract: offer, acceptance, consideration, and mutual assent; explain capacity and legality requirements; distinguish bilateral from unilateral contracts.
modules.readingNote: OER Business Law — Ch 8: Introduction to Contract Law
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Contract Law: Offer, Acceptance, and Consideration
modules.practiceNote: OER Business Law — Contract Formation Review
modules.assignment: Analyze three everyday scenarios (buying a coffee, signing a lease, clicking 'I agree' online) and for each, identify whether a valid contract exists by checking all required elements — offer, acceptance, consideration, capacity, and legality.

modules.title: Contract Law: Performance & Breach
modules.objectives: Distinguish complete, substantial, and material performance; explain conditions precedent, concurrent, and subsequent; identify types of breach (material, anticipatory, minor); understand discharge of contracts.
modules.readingNote: OER Business Law — Ch 9: Contract Performance, Breach, and Remedies
modules.practiceNote: OER Business Law — Breach of Contract Review
modules.assignment: Read a provided hypothetical construction contract dispute; identify whether the contractor's performance was complete, substantial, or material breach; explain what happens next under contract law.

modules.title: Contract Remedies
modules.objectives: Identify the goal of contract remedies (expectation damages); calculate compensatory, consequential, and liquidated damages; explain specific performance, rescission, and the duty to mitigate.
modules.readingNote: OER Business Law — Ch 9.3: Contract Remedies
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Contract Remedies: Damages, Specific Performance, Rescission
modules.practiceNote: OER Business Law — Contract Remedies Review
modules.assignment: A seller breaches a contract to deliver 1,000 widgets at $5 each; the buyer must purchase replacements on the open market for $7 each. Calculate compensatory damages, identify whether consequential damages might apply, and explain the duty to mitigate.

modules.title: Business Organizations: Sole Proprietorship & Partnership
modules.objectives: Describe the characteristics, advantages, and disadvantages of sole proprietorships and partnerships; understand personal liability exposure; explain general vs. limited partnerships and the Uniform Partnership Act.
modules.readingNote: OER Business Law — Ch 14: Forms of Business Organization
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Sole Proprietorships and Partnerships
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Business Structures: Sole Proprietorship vs Partnership
modules.practiceNote: OER Business Law — Business Organizations Review
modules.assignment: Two friends want to open a restaurant together. Analyze whether they should form a general partnership or a limited liability partnership; compare liability, management control, tax treatment, and ease of formation for each option.

modules.title: Corporations & LLCs
modules.objectives: Explain corporate formation, governance, and the corporate veil; distinguish S-corps, C-corps, and LLCs; understand shareholder rights; identify when courts pierce the corporate veil.
modules.readingNote: OER Business Law — Ch 14.2: Corporations and LLCs
modules.video2Note: YouTube — LLC vs Corporation: Which Is Right for Your Business?
modules.assignment: A tech startup founder asks for your advice: should they form a C-corp, S-corp, or LLC? Analyze their specific needs (seeking venture capital, 3 owners, wants to be taxed as a pass-through entity) and recommend the best structure with full justification.

modules.title: Employment & Labor Law
modules.objectives: Distinguish employees from independent contractors; explain at-will employment and its exceptions; describe major employment laws (Title VII, ADA, FLSA, FMLA); understand wrongful termination and workplace safety regulations.
modules.readingNote: OER Business Law — Ch 16: Employment and Labor Law
modules.video2Note: YouTube — US Employment Law: Key Concepts for Business
modules.practiceNote: OER Business Law — Employment Law Review
modules.assignment: A company fires an employee who reported a safety violation, alleging poor performance. Analyze whether this constitutes wrongful termination; identify which federal statute(s) may apply and what remedies the employee might seek.

modules.title: Intellectual Property: Patents, Trademarks & Copyright
modules.titleZh: 知识产权：专利、商标与版权
modules.objectives: Define patents, trademarks, trade secrets, and copyrights; explain what each protects and for how long; identify common IP violations; understand the process for registering trademarks with the USPTO.
modules.readingNote: OER Business Law — Ch 13: Intellectual Property
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Patents, Trademarks, and Copyrights Explained
modules.practiceNote: OER Business Law — Intellectual Property Review
modules.assignment: A student invents a new phone case. Analyze what IP protections are available — patent, trademark, trade secret, or copyright — for (a) the functional mechanism, (b) the design, (c) the brand name, and (d) the marketing slogan.

modules.title: Consumer Protection Law
modules.objectives: Identify major consumer protection statutes (FTC Act, TILA, FDCPA, CPSA); explain deceptive trade practices and the FTC's role; understand product liability theories (negligence, strict liability, warranty).
modules.readingNote: OER Business Law — Ch 15: Consumer Protection
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Consumer Protection Laws in the US
modules.practiceNote: OER Business Law — Consumer Protection Review
modules.assignment: Research a real product recall or FTC enforcement action from the past five years; identify which consumer protection statute(s) were involved, what the company did wrong, and what penalties or remedies resulted.

modules.title: Tort Law in Business
modules.objectives: Distinguish intentional torts, negligence, and strict liability; apply the four elements of negligence (duty, breach, causation, damages); explain defenses to negligence; understand tort liability for businesses and their employees.
modules.readingNote: OER Business Law — Ch 7: Introduction to Tort Law
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Negligence: The Four Elements Explained
modules.assignment: A customer slips on a wet floor in a grocery store and is injured. Apply the four elements of negligence (duty, breach, causation, damages) to analyze whether the store is liable; identify any defenses the store might raise.

modules.title: Criminal Law in Business
modules.objectives: Distinguish civil from criminal law consequences; identify white-collar crimes (fraud, embezzlement, bribery, insider trading); understand the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA); explain corporate criminal liability.
modules.readingNote: OER Business Law — Ch 6: Criminal Law
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Criminal and Civil Law
modules.video2Note: YouTube — White Collar Crime: Types and Famous Cases
modules.practiceNote: OER Business Law — Criminal Law in Business Review
modules.assignment: Research the Enron scandal or the Bernie Madoff Ponzi scheme; identify the criminal charges brought, the relevant statutes violated, the outcomes, and what corporate governance failures allowed the crime to occur.

modules.title: International Business Law
modules.objectives: Explain how international contracts differ from domestic ones; understand CISG, Incoterms, and currency risk; describe WTO dispute resolution; identify legal risks of doing business across borders.
modules.readingNote: OER Business Law — Ch 20: International Law
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — International Trade and Law
modules.practiceNote: OER Business Law — International Business Law Review
modules.assignment: A US company wants to export electronics to Germany. Identify the key legal considerations including the applicable contract law (CISG), payment terms (Incoterms), IP protection, and how disputes would be resolved.

modules.title: Dispute Resolution & Ethics
modules.objectives: Compare litigation, arbitration, and mediation as dispute resolution methods; explain the advantages and disadvantages of ADR for businesses; apply ethical frameworks to common business law scenarios.
modules.readingNote: OER Business Law — Ch 2: Legal and Ethical Environment of Business
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Mediation vs Arbitration vs Litigation
modules.practiceNote: OER Business Law — Dispute Resolution Review
modules.assignment: A business dispute involves $250,000 in contract damages. Compare and recommend the best dispute resolution method (litigation, mediation, or binding arbitration) considering cost, time, confidentiality, and the relationship between the parties.

quizQuestions.question: The highest source of law in the United States is:
options: Federal statutes passed by Congress
The US Constitution
State constitutions
Common law precedent
quizQuestions.answer: The US Constitution
quizQuestions.explanation: The Supremacy Clause establishes the US Constitution as the supreme law of the land — federal statutes, state laws, and court decisions must all be consistent with it.

quizQuestions.question: Civil law differs from criminal law primarily in that civil law:
options: Involves more serious offenses
Results in imprisonment as the standard penalty
Resolves disputes between private parties seeking compensation
Is prosecuted by the government on behalf of society
quizQuestions.answer: Resolves disputes between private parties seeking compensation
quizQuestions.explanation: Civil law handles disputes between private parties — individuals, businesses — seeking remedies like damages or injunctions. Criminal law involves the government prosecuting offenses against society.

quizQuestions.question: Administrative law is created by:
options: The President alone
Federal and state agencies through regulations and rules
Congress through constitutional amendments
The Supreme Court through precedent
quizQuestions.answer: Federal and state agencies through regulations and rules
quizQuestions.explanation: Administrative agencies (EPA, SEC, OSHA) create rules within the scope of authority delegated to them by Congress — these regulations have the force of law.

quizQuestions.question: Jurisdiction refers to a court's:
options: Ability to enforce international law
Authority to hear and decide a particular type of case
Power to create new legislation
Right to review executive orders
quizQuestions.answer: Authority to hear and decide a particular type of case
quizQuestions.explanation: Jurisdiction is the legal authority of a court to hear cases — determined by subject matter (what type of case) and personal jurisdiction (authority over the parties).

quizQuestions.question: Common law is best described as law that:
options: Is passed by state legislatures only
Is created by judges through court decisions that become precedent
Applies equally in all countries
Is found only in the US Constitution
quizQuestions.answer: Is created by judges through court decisions that become precedent
quizQuestions.explanation: Common law (case law) is judge-made law — courts interpret statutes and constitutional provisions, and their decisions become precedents binding on lower courts.

quizQuestions.question: The doctrine of stare decisis requires courts to:
options: Follow all international law precedents
Follow earlier decisions on the same legal issue (precedent)
Rule in favor of the government in all disputes
Decide cases based solely on statutory text
quizQuestions.answer: Follow earlier decisions on the same legal issue (precedent)
quizQuestions.explanation: Stare decisis ('to stand by things decided') promotes legal stability and predictability by requiring courts to follow established precedent from higher courts in the same jurisdiction.

quizQuestions.question: The US Supreme Court has original jurisdiction over cases involving:
options: All federal criminal cases
Disputes between two states
Small claims under $1,000
All contract disputes in the US
quizQuestions.answer: Disputes between two states
quizQuestions.explanation: The Constitution grants the Supreme Court original jurisdiction (the power to hear a case first) over disputes between states and cases involving foreign ambassadors.

quizQuestions.question: An appellate court's primary role is to:
options: Conduct new trials with new juries
Review the decisions of lower courts for legal errors
Hear evidence from witnesses for the first time
Set criminal sentences
quizQuestions.answer: Review the decisions of lower courts for legal errors
quizQuestions.explanation: Appellate courts do not retry cases — they review the trial record for legal errors (incorrect application of law) and may affirm, reverse, or remand the lower court's decision.

quizQuestions.question: Federal courts have subject matter jurisdiction over cases involving:
options: Any dispute worth more than $1,000
Questions of federal law, the US Constitution, or diversity of citizenship between states
Only criminal cases
Only cases where the government is a party
quizQuestions.answer: Questions of federal law, the US Constitution, or diversity of citizenship between states
quizQuestions.explanation: Federal courts have jurisdiction over federal questions (laws, treaties, Constitution) and diversity jurisdiction (parties from different states with claims over $75,000).

quizQuestions.question: When a higher court 'remands' a case, it means the court:
options: Upholds the lower court's decision completely
Dismisses the case permanently
Sends the case back to the lower court for further proceedings
Issues a final verdict on the merits
quizQuestions.answer: Sends the case back to the lower court for further proceedings
quizQuestions.explanation: A remand occurs when an appellate court returns the case to the trial court for further action consistent with the appellate court's ruling — often for a new trial or reconsideration.

quizQuestions.question: Consideration in contract law is best defined as:
options: The written terms of an agreement
Something of value exchanged by each party to support a contract
The intention of the parties to be legally bound
The formality of signing a written document
quizQuestions.answer: Something of value exchanged by each party to support a contract
quizQuestions.explanation: Consideration is the 'price' of the contract — what each party gives in exchange for the other's promise. Without mutual consideration, a promise is generally unenforceable.

quizQuestions.question: An offer in contract law must include:
options: A written signature by both parties
Definite terms and communication to the offeree
Only the price of the goods or services
Government approval
quizQuestions.answer: Definite terms and communication to the offeree
quizQuestions.explanation: A valid offer requires communication of sufficiently definite terms (who, what, price, quantity) to the intended offeree — so a contract could be formed upon acceptance.

quizQuestions.question: The mirror image rule states that acceptance must:
options: Be made in writing only
Exactly match the terms of the offer with no modifications
Occur within 24 hours of the offer
Be communicated by telephone only
quizQuestions.answer: Exactly match the terms of the offer with no modifications
quizQuestions.explanation: Under the common law mirror image rule, acceptance must match the offer exactly. Any change in terms creates a counteroffer rather than acceptance.

quizQuestions.question: A contract with a minor (person under 18) is generally:
options: Void and completely unenforceable by either party
Voidable at the minor's option
Binding on both parties since both signed it
Enforceable only if a parent co-signs
quizQuestions.answer: Voidable at the minor's option
quizQuestions.explanation: Minors lack full contractual capacity — they may disaffirm (avoid) a contract at any time before reaching majority. The adult party, however, is bound.

quizQuestions.question: A contract to commit an illegal act is:
options: Voidable at either party's option
Enforceable if both parties agree
Void and unenforceable
Valid as long as it is in writing
quizQuestions.answer: Void and unenforceable
quizQuestions.explanation: Contracts whose purpose is illegal (e.g., paying someone to commit a crime) lack the legality element and are void — courts will not enforce them, leaving both parties without legal remedy.

quizQuestions.question: Anticipatory repudiation occurs when:
options: A party partially performs but then stops
A party clearly indicates BEFORE the performance is due that they will not perform
Both parties agree to cancel the contract
A party performs late but adequately
quizQuestions.answer: A party clearly indicates BEFORE the performance is due that they will not perform
quizQuestions.explanation: Anticipatory repudiation gives the non-breaching party the immediate right to treat the contract as breached and sue for damages — they need not wait for the performance deadline.

quizQuestions.question: Substantial performance of a contract means:
options: Performing less than half of the contract's requirements
Performing all terms perfectly with zero deviations
Completing most of the contract with only minor deviations
Performing exactly half of the contract's terms
quizQuestions.answer: Completing most of the contract with only minor deviations
quizQuestions.explanation: Substantial performance falls just short of complete performance — the key benefit of the bargain is delivered with only minor, unintentional deviations. The breaching party may recover contract price minus cost to complete.

quizQuestions.question: A material breach of contract allows the non-breaching party to:
options: Demand only specific performance
Treat the contract as terminated and sue for full expectation damages
Continue performance and sue for reduced price only
Accept the performance with no legal remedy
quizQuestions.answer: Treat the contract as terminated and sue for full expectation damages
quizQuestions.explanation: A material breach is so significant that it defeats the contract's essential purpose — the innocent party may stop their own performance and sue for full damages.

quizQuestions.question: A contract may be discharged (terminated without breach) by:
options: Either party at any time, for any reason
Mutual agreement of both parties to cancel or modify the contract
Only the party who wrote the contract
Government order only
quizQuestions.answer: Mutual agreement of both parties to cancel or modify the contract
quizQuestions.explanation: Contracts can be discharged by full performance, mutual agreement (rescission, novation), impossibility of performance, or operation of law — mutual agreement is common in business.

quizQuestions.question: The doctrine of impossibility discharges a contract when:
options: Performance becomes merely difficult or more expensive
An unforeseen event makes performance objectively impossible
One party decides performance is no longer convenient
The market price of the goods changes significantly
quizQuestions.answer: An unforeseen event makes performance objectively impossible
quizQuestions.explanation: Impossibility discharges performance when an objective, unforeseen event (death, destruction of unique subject matter, supervening illegality) makes performance impossible — not merely unprofitable.

quizQuestions.question: The goal of contract damages is to:
options: Punish the breaching party severely
Put the non-breaching party in the position they would have been in had the contract been performed
Award the maximum possible damages to the plaintiff
Divide losses equally between both parties
quizQuestions.answer: Put the non-breaching party in the position they would have been in had the contract been performed
quizQuestions.explanation: Contract law is designed to compensate, not punish. Expectation damages aim to give the non-breaching party the 'benefit of the bargain' — the economic position they would have enjoyed with full performance.

quizQuestions.question: Specific performance as a remedy is typically ordered when:
options: The contract involves a large amount of money
The subject matter is unique and money damages are inadequate
The breaching party cannot afford to pay damages
Both parties agree that specific performance is appropriate
quizQuestions.answer: The subject matter is unique and money damages are inadequate
quizQuestions.explanation: Courts order specific performance (requiring the party to actually perform) when goods or property are unique — like a one-of-a-kind painting or real estate — making money damages an insufficient substitute.

quizQuestions.question: Liquidated damages clauses in contracts are enforceable when:
options: The amount is always set at $1,000 regardless of harm
Actual damages were difficult to estimate and the amount is a reasonable forecast of actual harm
The clause is included only to punish the breaching party
The amount equals triple the contract price
quizQuestions.answer: Actual damages were difficult to estimate and the amount is a reasonable forecast of actual harm
quizQuestions.explanation: Liquidated damages clauses — agreeing in advance on a damage amount — are enforceable if damages were hard to predict and the amount is a reasonable (not punitive) estimate of likely harm.

quizQuestions.question: The duty to mitigate in contract law requires the non-breaching party to:
options: Accept whatever partial performance the breaching party offers
Take reasonable steps to reduce losses after a breach
Immediately file a lawsuit upon any breach
Pay the breaching party's expenses
quizQuestions.answer: Take reasonable steps to reduce losses after a breach
quizQuestions.explanation: The duty to mitigate prevents inflated damage awards — the non-breaching party cannot recover for losses they could have reasonably avoided after learning of the breach.

quizQuestions.question: Consequential damages are recoverable in contract cases when:
options: They always equal three times the direct damages
They were foreseeable by the breaching party at the time of contracting
They arise from the non-breaching party's own actions
They are specifically excluded by statute
quizQuestions.answer: They were foreseeable by the breaching party at the time of contracting
quizQuestions.explanation: Under the Hadley v Baxendale rule, consequential (special) damages — losses beyond direct harm — are recoverable only if the breaching party knew or should have known about the special circumstances at the time of contracting.

quizQuestions.question: The key disadvantage of a sole proprietorship from a legal standpoint is:
options: It is very difficult to form
The owner has unlimited personal liability for business debts
It cannot enter into contracts
It must have a board of directors
quizQuestions.answer: The owner has unlimited personal liability for business debts
quizQuestions.explanation: A sole proprietorship has no legal separation between the owner and the business — if the business cannot pay its debts, creditors can reach the owner's personal assets.

quizQuestions.question: In a general partnership, each partner is liable for:
options: Only their own share of the debts proportional to their ownership
Only the debts they personally incurred
The entire debt of the partnership (joint and several liability)
No personal liability, like a corporation
quizQuestions.answer: The entire debt of the partnership (joint and several liability)
quizQuestions.explanation: In a general partnership, all partners have unlimited personal liability — each partner can be held responsible for 100% of partnership debts, not just their ownership percentage.

quizQuestions.question: A limited partnership requires at least:
options: Three general partners and no limited partners
One general partner (unlimited liability) and one limited partner (liability limited to investment)
Five shareholders and a board of directors
Government licensing before formation
quizQuestions.answer: One general partner (unlimited liability) and one limited partner (liability limited to investment)
quizQuestions.explanation: A limited partnership has two classes: general partners who manage the business and bear unlimited liability, and limited partners who invest but cannot manage and risk only their capital contribution.

quizQuestions.question: A partnership agreement primarily serves to:
options: Register the partnership with the federal government
Define the rights, duties, profit-sharing, and decision-making procedures among partners
Limit partners' personal liability
Determine tax rates for the partnership
quizQuestions.answer: Define the rights, duties, profit-sharing, and decision-making procedures among partners
quizQuestions.explanation: A partnership agreement customizes the partnership's governance — without it, the default rules of the Uniform Partnership Act (UPA) apply, which may not match the partners' intentions.

quizQuestions.question: Compared to a corporation, a sole proprietorship is typically preferred for small businesses because it:
options: Offers greater liability protection
Is simpler and less expensive to form and operate
Has more access to capital markets
Provides superior tax advantages
quizQuestions.answer: Is simpler and less expensive to form and operate
quizQuestions.explanation: Sole proprietorships require no formal filing in most cases — they begin when the owner starts doing business. No bylaws, board of directors, or complex formation documents are needed.

quizQuestions.question: The 'corporate veil' concept means that:
options: Corporations must keep their finances secret from shareholders
Shareholders are generally not personally liable for corporate debts
Corporate officers are immune from prosecution
Corporations can hide their ownership structure
quizQuestions.answer: Shareholders are generally not personally liable for corporate debts
quizQuestions.explanation: The corporate veil separates the legal identity of the corporation from its shareholders — shareholders' maximum loss is their investment, not their personal assets.

quizQuestions.question: Courts will 'pierce the corporate veil' (hold shareholders personally liable) when:
options: The corporation becomes very profitable
The corporation is used as a fraud or when corporate formalities are completely ignored
The corporation has fewer than 10 employees
The shareholders are foreign nationals
quizQuestions.answer: The corporation is used as a fraud or when corporate formalities are completely ignored
quizQuestions.explanation: Piercing the corporate veil is an equitable remedy applied when shareholders abuse the corporate form by treating the corporation as their personal bank or using it to commit fraud.

quizQuestions.question: An LLC (Limited Liability Company) combines the liability protection of a corporation with the tax treatment of:
options: A C-corporation (double taxation)
A partnership or sole proprietorship (pass-through taxation)
A nonprofit organization
A foreign corporation
quizQuestions.answer: A partnership or sole proprietorship (pass-through taxation)
quizQuestions.explanation: LLCs offer limited liability for members while allowing profits to pass through directly to members' personal tax returns — avoiding the double taxation of C-corporations.

quizQuestions.question: An S-Corporation differs from a C-Corporation primarily in that an S-Corp:
options: Has no shareholders
Can have unlimited numbers of shareholders
Has pass-through taxation (no corporate-level income tax)
Is required to be publicly traded
quizQuestions.answer: Has pass-through taxation (no corporate-level income tax)
quizQuestions.explanation: S-Corps elect to pass corporate income, losses, deductions, and credits through to shareholders for federal tax purposes — avoiding the double taxation that applies to C-Corps.

quizQuestions.question: A corporation is governed by which of the following documents that define its structure and operations?
options: A partnership agreement and operating agreement
Articles of incorporation and corporate bylaws
A franchise agreement and license
A business plan and pitch deck
quizQuestions.answer: Articles of incorporation and corporate bylaws
quizQuestions.explanation: Articles of incorporation are the foundational formation documents filed with the state; bylaws are the internal rules governing day-to-day operations, meetings, and governance.

quizQuestions.question: Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 prohibits employment discrimination based on:
options: Age and disability
Race, color, religion, sex, and national origin
Sexual orientation and gender identity only
Salary level and job title
quizQuestions.answer: Race, color, religion, sex, and national origin
quizQuestions.explanation: Title VII is the cornerstone federal anti-discrimination law prohibiting employment discrimination in hiring, firing, compensation, and terms of employment based on five protected characteristics.

quizQuestions.question: At-will employment means that:
options: Employees can only be fired after a court hearing
Either party may end the employment relationship at any time for any lawful reason
Employers must provide six months' notice before termination
Employees must give one year's notice before resigning
quizQuestions.answer: Either party may end the employment relationship at any time for any lawful reason
quizQuestions.explanation: At-will employment (the US default) allows employers to fire and employees to quit at any time without cause — but with important statutory exceptions (cannot fire for discriminatory or retaliatory reasons).

quizQuestions.question: The Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) establishes:
options: Rules for international trade unions
Minimum wage, overtime pay, and child labor standards
Employee stock option requirements
Mandatory employer healthcare contributions
quizQuestions.answer: Minimum wage, overtime pay, and child labor standards
quizQuestions.explanation: The FLSA sets the federal minimum wage, requires overtime pay (1.5× regular rate for hours over 40/week), and restricts child labor — foundational US employment law.

quizQuestions.question: An independent contractor differs from an employee in that:
options: Independent contractors receive full employee benefits
Independent contractors control how and when they work and are not subject to the employer's direction
Employees are always paid more than contractors
Independent contractors must work on-site
quizQuestions.answer: Independent contractors control how and when they work and are not subject to the employer's direction
quizQuestions.explanation: The employee vs. independent contractor distinction turns on the degree of control — contractors control their work method; employers control employees' time, place, and manner of work.

quizQuestions.question: OSHA (Occupational Safety and Health Administration) requires employers to:
options: Provide free health insurance to all employees
Provide a safe workplace free from recognized hazards
Pay all overtime at twice the regular rate
Allow unlimited sick leave for all employees
quizQuestions.answer: Provide a safe workplace free from recognized hazards
quizQuestions.explanation: OSHA's general duty clause requires employers to provide employment free from recognized hazards likely to cause death or serious harm — and to comply with specific OSHA safety standards.

quizQuestions.question: A utility patent protects:
options: The ornamental appearance of a product
A new, useful, and non-obvious process, machine, manufacture, or composition of matter
A company's brand name and logo
Creative works like books and songs
quizQuestions.answer: A new, useful, and non-obvious process, machine, manufacture, or composition of matter
quizQuestions.explanation: Utility patents protect functional inventions for 20 years from filing — the invention must be novel, useful, and non-obvious to qualify.

quizQuestions.question: A trademark protects:
options: Functional aspects of a product
Creative expression such as literature and music
A word, symbol, or design that identifies the source of goods or services
Confidential business information
quizQuestions.answer: A word, symbol, or design that identifies the source of goods or services
quizQuestions.explanation: Trademarks protect brand identifiers — names, logos, slogans — that distinguish one company's goods or services from competitors', preventing consumer confusion about origin.

quizQuestions.question: Copyright protection begins:
options: Only after registration with the US Copyright Office
When the work is created and fixed in a tangible medium
After the work is published commercially
Upon the creator's death
quizQuestions.answer: When the work is created and fixed in a tangible medium
quizQuestions.explanation: Under US law, copyright arises automatically when an original creative work is created and fixed in a tangible form — registration is not required for protection, though it provides benefits in litigation.

quizQuestions.question: A trade secret is protected under law as long as:
options: The owner files for protection with the USPTO every 10 years
Reasonable steps are taken to maintain its secrecy
It is patented within two years of discovery
Only one person knows the secret
quizQuestions.answer: Reasonable steps are taken to maintain its secrecy
quizQuestions.explanation: Trade secrets (formulas, processes, business strategies) are protected indefinitely as long as they are not publicly known and the owner takes reasonable measures to keep them confidential.

quizQuestions.question: Trademark infringement occurs when:
options: Two companies sell products at the same price
A company uses a mark that is likely to cause consumer confusion about the source of goods
A company registers a trademark internationally
A company uses a generic word as a trademark
quizQuestions.answer: A company uses a mark that is likely to cause consumer confusion about the source of goods
quizQuestions.explanation: The test for trademark infringement is 'likelihood of confusion' — whether a reasonable consumer might be confused about who makes or endorses the product.

quizQuestions.question: The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) has authority to:
options: Set corporate tax rates
Prohibit unfair or deceptive acts and practices in commerce
Regulate securities markets and stock trading
Set interest rates for consumer loans
quizQuestions.answer: Prohibit unfair or deceptive acts and practices in commerce
quizQuestions.explanation: The FTC Act empowers the FTC to protect consumers from deceptive advertising, unfair business practices, identity theft, and anti-competitive behavior.

quizQuestions.question: Strict product liability holds a manufacturer liable when:
options: The product causes harm only if the manufacturer was negligent
A defective product causes harm even if the manufacturer took all reasonable care
Consumers misuse the product in any way
The product has expired
quizQuestions.answer: A defective product causes harm even if the manufacturer took all reasonable care
quizQuestions.explanation: Strict liability removes the need to prove negligence — if a product has a manufacturing, design, or warning defect that causes injury, the manufacturer is liable regardless of the level of care taken.

quizQuestions.question: Truth in Lending Act (TILA) requires lenders to:
options: Charge interest rates approved by Congress
Clearly disclose all credit terms including APR before a consumer signs a credit agreement
Only lend money to consumers with excellent credit
Register with the FTC before offering loans
quizQuestions.answer: Clearly disclose all credit terms including APR before a consumer signs a credit agreement
quizQuestions.explanation: TILA's disclosure requirements help consumers compare credit offers by mandating standardized disclosure of APR, fees, total cost of credit, and other key terms before the agreement is signed.

quizQuestions.question: An implied warranty of merchantability means that a product sold by a merchant:
options: Comes with a lifetime guarantee
Will perform safely and as expected for its ordinary purpose
Has been personally endorsed by the seller
Has received FDA approval
quizQuestions.answer: Will perform safely and as expected for its ordinary purpose
quizQuestions.explanation: Under the UCC, merchants automatically warrant that goods are fit for their ordinary use — a toaster must toast bread, a car must be drivable — without any express promise.

quizQuestions.question: A class action lawsuit in consumer protection cases allows:
options: Only one consumer to sue a company for damages affecting many
A large group of similarly harmed consumers to sue as a collective group
Companies to sue consumers in groups
The government to punish consumers for fraud
quizQuestions.answer: A large group of similarly harmed consumers to sue as a collective group
quizQuestions.explanation: Class actions aggregate the claims of many similarly situated plaintiffs — practical when individual damages are small but total harm is large — making litigation economically feasible.

quizQuestions.question: The four elements of negligence a plaintiff must prove are:
options: Intent, action, result, and damage
Duty, breach, causation, and damages
Warning, risk, harm, and liability
Fault, injury, loss, and remedy
quizQuestions.answer: Duty, breach, causation, and damages
quizQuestions.explanation: To win a negligence claim, a plaintiff must prove: (1) the defendant had a duty of care, (2) the defendant breached that duty, (3) the breach caused the plaintiff's injury, and (4) the plaintiff suffered damages.

quizQuestions.question: Respondeat superior is a doctrine that holds employers liable for:
options: Their own personal negligence in managing the business
The torts of their employees committed within the scope of employment
All customer injuries regardless of negligence
Contract breaches by independent contractors
quizQuestions.answer: The torts of their employees committed within the scope of employment
quizQuestions.explanation: Respondeat superior ('let the master answer') makes employers vicariously liable for torts committed by employees acting within the course and scope of their employment.

quizQuestions.question: Intentional torts in a business context include:
options: Accidental product defects
Fraud, defamation, and intentional interference with contract
Injuries from natural disasters at a business
Regulatory violations only
quizQuestions.answer: Fraud, defamation, and intentional interference with contract
quizQuestions.explanation: Intentional torts require deliberate wrongful acts — fraud (intentional misrepresentation), defamation (false statements harming reputation), and tortious interference are common business intentional torts.

quizQuestions.question: Comparative negligence reduces a plaintiff's damages based on:
options: The defendant's financial ability to pay
The plaintiff's own percentage of fault for the injury
The number of defendants in the case
The severity of the injury only
quizQuestions.answer: The plaintiff's own percentage of fault for the injury
quizQuestions.explanation: Comparative negligence apportions fault between plaintiff and defendant — if the plaintiff is 30% at fault, their damages award is reduced by 30%.

quizQuestions.question: The 'reasonable person' standard in negligence law asks whether:
options: An expert in the relevant field would have acted differently
An ordinary person of average prudence would have acted the same way in similar circumstances
The most careful person imaginable would have prevented the harm
The defendant personally knew the action was dangerous
quizQuestions.answer: An ordinary person of average prudence would have acted the same way in similar circumstances
quizQuestions.explanation: The reasonable person standard is an objective test — not what this particular defendant knew or intended, but how a reasonably prudent person would have behaved under the same circumstances.

quizQuestions.question: White-collar crime is best described as:
options: Violent crimes committed by executives
Non-violent crimes committed for financial gain in business or professional contexts
Any crime committed by a person wearing professional attire
Crimes that only affect the government
quizQuestions.answer: Non-violent crimes committed for financial gain in business or professional contexts
quizQuestions.explanation: White-collar crime encompasses fraud, embezzlement, bribery, insider trading, and similar non-violent financial offenses typically committed by persons in positions of trust.

quizQuestions.question: Insider trading involves:
options: Trading stocks on a public exchange
Buying or selling securities based on material non-public information in violation of a duty
Trading between employees within a company
Using publicly available analyst reports for investment decisions
quizQuestions.answer: Buying or selling securities based on material non-public information in violation of a duty
quizQuestions.explanation: Insider trading — trading on confidential, non-public information — violates securities law by giving traders an unfair advantage over the public market and undermining market integrity.

quizQuestions.question: The Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA) prohibits US companies from:
options: Conducting business in foreign countries without a license
Bribing foreign government officials to obtain or retain business
Importing foreign goods without paying tariffs
Hiring foreign nationals for domestic operations
quizQuestions.answer: Bribing foreign government officials to obtain or retain business
quizQuestions.explanation: The FCPA makes it illegal for US companies and their agents to bribe foreign officials for business advantages — and requires accurate books and records regardless of overseas operations.

quizQuestions.question: Embezzlement is a crime involving:
options: Breaking and entering a business premises
Misappropriation of funds or property by a person entrusted with them
Forging a company's signatures on documents
Selling confidential business information to competitors
quizQuestions.answer: Misappropriation of funds or property by a person entrusted with them
quizQuestions.explanation: Embezzlement is theft by someone in a fiduciary position — an employee, officer, or trustee who diverts funds or property entrusted to them for personal gain.

quizQuestions.question: The burden of proof in a criminal business case is:
options: Preponderance of the evidence (more likely than not)
Clear and convincing evidence
Beyond a reasonable doubt
Absolute certainty
quizQuestions.answer: Beyond a reasonable doubt
quizQuestions.explanation: Criminal cases require proof beyond a reasonable doubt — the highest standard of proof in the US legal system — because criminal conviction carries potential imprisonment and severe consequences.

quizQuestions.question: The CISG (UN Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods) governs:
options: Employment disputes between countries
Sales contracts between parties in different signatory countries
Intellectual property licensing internationally
Foreign direct investment disputes
quizQuestions.answer: Sales contracts between parties in different signatory countries
quizQuestions.explanation: The CISG is an international treaty providing uniform rules for commercial contracts between businesses in different signatory countries — often applying by default unless parties opt out.

quizQuestions.question: Incoterms are international trade terms that define:
options: The applicable interest rates for international loans
The responsibilities of buyers and sellers for delivery, risk, and costs in international trade
Immigration requirements for business travelers
Currency exchange rates between countries
quizQuestions.answer: The responsibilities of buyers and sellers for delivery, risk, and costs in international trade
quizQuestions.explanation: Incoterms (International Commercial Terms) published by the ICC are standardized three-letter codes (FOB, CIF, EXW) that specify who bears risk and responsibility at each stage of international shipment.

quizQuestions.question: Country risk in international business refers to:
options: Currency fluctuations that affect trade profitability
The risk that a foreign government's actions (nationalization, sanctions, instability) will harm business
The risk of intellectual property theft in international markets
Competition from foreign firms in domestic markets
quizQuestions.answer: The risk that a foreign government's actions (nationalization, sanctions, instability) will harm business
quizQuestions.explanation: Country (political) risk encompasses the risk that political instability, expropriation, regulatory changes, or sanctions in a foreign country will disrupt or destroy business operations.

quizQuestions.question: The World Trade Organization (WTO) serves primarily to:
options: Set tax rates for multinational corporations
Provide a framework for international trade rules and resolve trade disputes between nations
Regulate international currency exchange
Enforce intellectual property rights globally
quizQuestions.answer: Provide a framework for international trade rules and resolve trade disputes between nations
quizQuestions.explanation: The WTO administers trade agreements, provides a forum for trade negotiations, and has a dispute settlement system for resolving conflicts between member countries' trade policies.

quizQuestions.question: A choice of law clause in an international contract specifies:
options: Which language the contract is written in
Which country's law will govern the contract's interpretation and enforcement
The currency in which payment will be made
How many arbitrators will resolve disputes
quizQuestions.answer: Which country's law will govern the contract's interpretation and enforcement
quizQuestions.explanation: Choice of law clauses prevent uncertainty by specifying upfront which jurisdiction's laws apply — critical in international contracts where parties and performance span multiple legal systems.

quizQuestions.question: Mediation differs from arbitration in that mediation:
options: Results in a binding decision issued by a neutral third party
Uses a neutral facilitator to help parties reach a voluntary agreement
Is always more expensive than litigation
Requires a judge to preside over the proceedings
quizQuestions.answer: Uses a neutral facilitator to help parties reach a voluntary agreement
quizQuestions.explanation: In mediation, a neutral mediator facilitates negotiation but has no power to impose a decision — settlement is voluntary. In arbitration, the arbitrator issues a binding decision.

quizQuestions.question: Arbitration is generally preferred over litigation by businesses because it is typically:
options: Free of charge and open to the public
Faster, cheaper, more confidential, and the arbitrator has subject-matter expertise
Always decided in the company's favor
More likely to result in punitive damages
quizQuestions.answer: Faster, cheaper, more confidential, and the arbitrator has subject-matter expertise
quizQuestions.explanation: Arbitration's advantages include speed (no court backlog), lower cost, privacy (proceedings and results stay confidential), and specialized expertise of commercial arbitrators.

quizQuestions.question: The utilitarian approach to business ethics makes decisions based on:
options: Strict adherence to moral duties regardless of consequences
Maximizing overall well-being and minimizing harm for the greatest number of people
Whatever benefits the decision-maker personally
Following the specific rules of the industry
quizQuestions.answer: Maximizing overall well-being and minimizing harm for the greatest number of people
quizQuestions.explanation: Utilitarian ethics evaluates actions by their consequences — an action is ethical if it produces the greatest good for the greatest number of people affected.

quizQuestions.question: A mandatory arbitration clause in a consumer contract is controversial because it:
options: Always results in outcomes favorable to consumers
Often prevents consumers from joining class actions and limits their legal options
Requires consumers to pay no fees for arbitration
Is illegal under US federal law
quizQuestions.answer: Often prevents consumers from joining class actions and limits their legal options
quizQuestions.explanation: Mandatory arbitration clauses — common in consumer agreements — compel individual arbitration and frequently waive class action rights, reducing consumers' practical ability to challenge corporate misconduct.

quizQuestions.question: Corporate social responsibility (CSR) in a legal context refers to:
options: A company's legal obligation to maximize shareholder profit alone
Voluntary actions companies take to meet ethical, social, and environmental obligations beyond legal requirements
Government regulations requiring companies to donate to charity
The obligation to pay the highest possible wages by law
quizQuestions.answer: Voluntary actions companies take to meet ethical, social, and environmental obligations beyond legal requirements
quizQuestions.explanation: CSR goes beyond legal compliance — companies voluntarily adopt practices that benefit stakeholders (employees, communities, environment) as part of their broader social obligation.

questions.question: Which of the following is a source of law in the United States?
options: Corporate bylaws
Industry customs
Administrative regulations issued by federal agencies
International business norms
questions.answer: Administrative regulations issued by federal agencies
questions.explanation: Administrative regulations created by agencies like the EPA, SEC, or OSHA within their congressional mandate have the force of law and are binding on businesses and individuals.

questions.question: A contract requires consideration from both parties. Which of the following is valid consideration?
options: A promise to make a gift with no condition
A pre-existing legal duty to pay taxes
$1,000 paid by the buyer in exchange for a seller's product
Love and affection between family members
questions.answer: $1,000 paid by the buyer in exchange for a seller's product
questions.explanation: Money paid in exchange for goods is classic bilateral consideration — each party gives something of value (money and product), satisfying the consideration requirement.

questions.question: Under the doctrine of stare decisis, a trial court in the 9th Circuit must follow decisions from:
options: Any federal district court in the country
The 9th Circuit Court of Appeals and the US Supreme Court
Only the US Supreme Court
Any state supreme court in the 9th Circuit
questions.answer: The 9th Circuit Court of Appeals and the US Supreme Court
questions.explanation: Stare decisis requires lower courts to follow decisions from courts above them in the same judicial hierarchy — trial courts in the 9th Circuit follow both the 9th Circuit Court of Appeals and the Supreme Court.

questions.question: A contract that lacks consideration is generally:
options: Voidable at the breaching party's option
Enforceable if it is in writing
Unenforceable
Enforceable if both parties signed it
questions.answer: Unenforceable
questions.explanation: Consideration is a required element of a valid contract — without mutual exchange of value, a promise is a gift that courts will not enforce.

questions.question: Material breach of a contract entitles the non-breaching party to:
options: Only partial damages for the inconvenience
Treat the contract as discharged and sue for full expectation damages
Continue performing and accept partial payment
Renegotiate the original contract terms
questions.answer: Treat the contract as discharged and sue for full expectation damages
questions.explanation: A material breach defeats the essential purpose of the contract — the innocent party is excused from further performance and can pursue the full expectation interest.

questions.question: Which of the following is a defense against a negligence claim?
options: The defendant is a corporation, not an individual
The plaintiff was also negligent (comparative negligence)
The harm occurred at work rather than at home
The defendant had no insurance
questions.answer: The plaintiff was also negligent (comparative negligence)
questions.explanation: Comparative negligence reduces or eliminates the defendant's liability based on the plaintiff's own contribution to the harm — the plaintiff's damages are reduced proportionally to their fault.

questions.question: The primary advantage of forming an LLC for a small business is:
options: Unlimited ability to raise money from public investors
Limited personal liability combined with pass-through taxation
Avoidance of all state and federal taxes
Simple formation requiring no legal documents
questions.answer: Limited personal liability combined with pass-through taxation
questions.explanation: LLCs combine the liability protection of a corporation with the tax simplicity of a partnership — members are not personally liable for business debts, and profits are taxed only at the member level.

questions.question: An employee fired immediately after reporting workplace safety violations to OSHA would most likely have a claim for:
options: Voluntary resignation
Wrongful termination in violation of public policy (retaliation)
Breach of contract if at-will employed
Discrimination under the ADA
questions.answer: Wrongful termination in violation of public policy (retaliation)
questions.explanation: Federal and state whistleblower protection statutes prohibit retaliation against employees who report legal violations — terminating an employee for OSHA reporting is retaliatory wrongful termination.

questions.question: Which type of intellectual property protection has the shortest term of protection?
options: Copyright
Utility patent
Trademark
Trade secret
questions.answer: Utility patent
questions.explanation: Utility patents protect inventions for 20 years from the filing date — shorter than copyright (life + 70 years) and trademarks (indefinitely renewable). Trade secrets can last forever if secrecy is maintained.

questions.question: In a general partnership, if Partner A makes a contract with a supplier on behalf of the partnership:
options: Only Partner A is bound by the contract
The partnership and all general partners are bound by the contract
Only the partners who approved the contract are bound
The contract is void without all partners' signatures
questions.answer: The partnership and all general partners are bound by the contract
questions.explanation: Each partner is an agent of the partnership — acts within the ordinary scope of partnership business bind the partnership and all general partners personally.

questions.question: Anticipatory repudiation differs from ordinary breach in that anticipatory repudiation:
options: Occurs after the performance deadline has passed
Occurs before the performance is due when a party clearly refuses to perform
Only applies to real estate contracts
Requires written notice to be effective
questions.answer: Occurs before the performance is due when a party clearly refuses to perform
questions.explanation: Anticipatory repudiation allows the innocent party to immediately treat the contract as breached and mitigate damages — they need not wait until the actual performance date.

questions.question: Which type of business organization avoids double taxation?
options: C-Corporation
Publicly traded corporation
Limited Liability Company (LLC)
B-Corporation
questions.answer: Limited Liability Company (LLC)
questions.explanation: LLCs use pass-through taxation — profits are reported on members' individual returns and taxed only once. C-corporations face double taxation: corporate income tax plus shareholder dividend tax.

questions.question: Title VII employment discrimination protection applies to employers with:
options: Any number of employees
15 or more employees
At least 50 employees
100 or more employees
questions.answer: 15 or more employees
questions.explanation: Title VII applies to employers with 15 or more employees — a threshold that exempts very small businesses while covering the vast majority of US workers.

questions.question: Expectation damages in contract law are designed to:
options: Return both parties to their pre-contract positions
Give the plaintiff the benefit of the bargain — the economic position they would have had if the contract was performed
Punish the breaching party for misconduct
Cover only the plaintiff's out-of-pocket expenses
questions.answer: Give the plaintiff the benefit of the bargain — the economic position they would have had if the contract was performed
questions.explanation: Expectation (benefit-of-the-bargain) damages are the standard contract remedy — they compensate for the value the plaintiff would have received from full performance.

questions.question: Which federal agency primarily enforces consumer protection laws against deceptive advertising?
options: Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)
Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
Department of Justice (DOJ)
questions.answer: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
questions.explanation: The FTC is the primary federal consumer protection agency — it enforces laws against deceptive advertising, unfair business practices, and false product claims.

questions.question: The 'piercing of the corporate veil' doctrine holds shareholders personally liable when:
options: A corporation earns very high profits
The corporation is used as a tool for fraud or completely ignores corporate formalities
A corporation is publicly traded
A corporation has fewer than five shareholders
questions.answer: The corporation is used as a tool for fraud or completely ignores corporate formalities
questions.explanation: Courts pierce the veil when the corporation is a sham — used to commit fraud, lacks separate finances from owners, or fails to observe basic corporate formalities — exposing shareholders to personal liability.

questions.question: Under the FCPA, a US company that discovers its foreign subsidiary has been bribing officials should:
options: Continue the practice as long as it is legal in the foreign country
Immediately stop the practice, investigate, and potentially self-disclose to the DOJ/SEC
Report the activity only to local foreign authorities
Ignore the practice since it occurs overseas
questions.answer: Immediately stop the practice, investigate, and potentially self-disclose to the DOJ/SEC
questions.explanation: FCPA liability extends to subsidiaries and agents of US companies globally. Self-disclosure, cooperation, and remediation are critical mitigating factors in DOJ/SEC enforcement decisions.

questions.question: Specific performance is most commonly granted as a contract remedy in cases involving:
options: Service contracts for routine work
Sale of consumer goods under $500
Real estate sales and sales of unique goods
Employment contracts
questions.answer: Real estate sales and sales of unique goods
questions.explanation: Because every parcel of real estate is unique and money cannot perfectly substitute, courts routinely grant specific performance in real estate sale contracts where the seller refuses to close.

questions.question: A business owner who operates as a sole proprietor but also has personal savings of $200,000 should understand that:
options: Their personal savings are fully protected from business creditors
Their personal savings could be seized to satisfy business debts
They can convert to an LLC at any time with no tax consequences
Business debts can never exceed the business's revenue
questions.answer: Their personal savings could be seized to satisfy business debts
questions.explanation: Sole proprietorships provide no liability separation — all personal assets are fully exposed to claims by business creditors.

questions.question: The doctrine of respondeat superior means a company is liable when its employee:
options: Commits a tort while on a personal errand unrelated to work
Commits a tort while acting within the scope of their employment duties
Is fired for poor performance
Signs a contract on behalf of the company without authority
questions.answer: Commits a tort while acting within the scope of their employment duties
questions.explanation: Respondeat superior imputes the torts of employees to employers when the wrongful act occurs within the course and scope of employment — e.g., a delivery driver causing an accident during a delivery.

questions.question: Which of the following would be protected as a trade secret?
options: A publicly published recipe in a cookbook
The formula for Coca-Cola kept strictly confidential for over 100 years
A business idea that has been discussed at public industry conferences
A logo registered with the USPTO
questions.answer: The formula for Coca-Cola kept strictly confidential for over 100 years
questions.explanation: A trade secret is information with economic value that is actively kept secret — Coca-Cola's formula is the most famous example, protected indefinitely through strict confidentiality measures.

questions.question: In international commercial arbitration, the 'seat' of arbitration determines:
options: Where the arbitrators physically sit during hearings
The law governing the arbitration proceedings and where the award can be enforced
Which language the proceedings are conducted in
How many arbitrators will participate
questions.answer: The law governing the arbitration proceedings and where the award can be enforced
questions.explanation: The legal seat (place) of arbitration determines which national law governs the arbitration procedure and which courts have supervisory jurisdiction over the proceedings and award.

questions.question: The Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) requires employers to provide:
options: Free medical care to disabled employees
Reasonable accommodations for qualified employees with disabilities unless it causes undue hardship
A disability pay supplement for all disabled workers
Disability insurance for every employee
questions.answer: Reasonable accommodations for qualified employees with disabilities unless it causes undue hardship
questions.explanation: The ADA requires employers to make reasonable adjustments (flexible hours, modified duties, assistive technology) to enable qualified disabled employees to perform essential job functions.

questions.question: Strict product liability is advantageous for injured plaintiffs because they do not need to prove:
options: That they were injured
That a defect existed in the product
That the manufacturer was negligent
That the product caused the injury
questions.answer: That the manufacturer was negligent
questions.explanation: Strict liability removes negligence from the plaintiff's burden of proof — they must only show the product was defective and caused injury, not that the manufacturer failed to exercise reasonable care.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between mediation and litigation in resolving a business dispute?
options: Mediation always produces a binding result; litigation does not
Mediation is conducted by government judges; litigation uses private neutrals
Mediation is voluntary and non-binding unless settled; litigation produces a binding court judgment
Mediation costs more than litigation for most disputes
questions.answer: Mediation is voluntary and non-binding unless settled; litigation produces a binding court judgment
questions.explanation: Mediation facilitates voluntary negotiation — parties control the outcome and can walk away. Litigation results in a binding court judgment imposed by a judge or jury.

questions.question: An employee working 50 hours per week at $20/hour (non-exempt under FLSA) should receive total weekly pay of:
questions.explanation: FLSA requires non-exempt employees to receive 1.5× the regular rate for hours over 40: 40 × $20 = $800 + 10 × $30 = $300 → total $1,100.

questions.question: A copyright holder's exclusive rights include all of the following EXCEPT:
options: The right to reproduce the work
The right to create derivative works
The right to control all private discussion of the work
The right to distribute copies publicly
questions.answer: The right to control all private discussion of the work
questions.explanation: Copyright grants reproduction, distribution, derivative work, public display, and performance rights — but does not control private conversations, criticism, commentary, or other First Amendment-protected speech about the work.

questions.question: White-collar crimes like fraud and embezzlement can result in both:
options: Only civil liability for restitution
Only criminal prosecution and imprisonment
Both criminal prosecution (imprisonment, fines) and civil liability (damages to victims)
Only regulatory fines from the SEC or FTC
questions.answer: Both criminal prosecution (imprisonment, fines) and civil liability (damages to victims)
questions.explanation: White-collar crimes expose defendants to parallel civil and criminal proceedings — criminal prosecution by the government AND civil suits by harmed investors, customers, or partners.

questions.question: A choice of forum clause in an international business contract specifies:
options: The language the contract must be written in
The court or arbitral tribunal in which disputes must be resolved
The currency to be used for payment
The country's tax law that applies to the contract
questions.answer: The court or arbitral tribunal in which disputes must be resolved
questions.explanation: Choice of forum (jurisdiction) clauses designate where disputes will be heard — preventing costly disputes over venue and ensuring predictability in international contracts.

questions.question: The stakeholder theory of corporate ethics (as contrasted with shareholder primacy) argues that businesses owe duties to:
options: Shareholders alone, as owners of the business
All parties with a stake in the business, including employees, customers, suppliers, and communities
Only the government through regulatory compliance
International customers and employees only
questions.answer: All parties with a stake in the business, including employees, customers, suppliers, and communities
questions.explanation: Stakeholder theory holds that corporations should balance the interests of all affected parties — not just maximize shareholder returns — reflecting the evolving understanding of business's role in society.

questions.question: Which factor is NOT used to determine whether a worker is an employee or an independent contractor under most tests?
options: The degree of control the hiring party has over how work is done
Whether the worker uses their own tools and equipment
The worker's race and national origin
Whether the work is a core part of the hiring party's business
questions.answer: The worker's race and national origin
questions.explanation: Employee vs. contractor tests look at economic realities and control — they never consider protected characteristics like race or national origin, which are irrelevant to the classification and protected from discrimination.

questions.question: Binding arbitration, as opposed to non-binding arbitration, means that:
options: The parties can ignore the arbitrator's decision
The arbitrator's award is final and enforceable in court, with very limited grounds for appeal
Only the plaintiff is bound by the decision
The arbitrator must be a licensed judge
questions.answer: The arbitrator's award is final and enforceable in court, with very limited grounds for appeal
questions.explanation: Binding arbitration produces a final award that courts will enforce under the Federal Arbitration Act — appealable only on very narrow grounds like fraud or arbitrator misconduct.

questions.question: A company's compliance program is most effective at preventing criminal liability when it:
options: Is a document written to look good without real enforcement
Includes clear policies, regular training, confidential reporting channels, and genuine enforcement from leadership
Only focuses on financial reporting requirements
Is managed by the same department it is designed to oversee
questions.answer: Includes clear policies, regular training, confidential reporting channels, and genuine enforcement from leadership
questions.explanation: The DOJ's guidelines treat effective compliance programs as mitigating factors in corporate criminal cases — they must be genuinely implemented, not cosmetic.

questions.question: Consequential damages in a contract dispute are limited by the rule that they must have been:
options: Incurred within 30 days of the breach
Foreseeable by the breaching party at the time of contracting
Specified exactly in the written contract
Approved by a court before the breach occurred
questions.answer: Foreseeable by the breaching party at the time of contracting
questions.explanation: The Hadley v Baxendale foreseeability rule limits consequential damages — the breaching party is liable only for losses they could reasonably have anticipated when they entered the contract.

questions.question: Under strict product liability, which of the following product defects would make a manufacturer liable?
options: A product used by the consumer in a way no one could have predicted
A manufacturing defect where one unit was assembled incorrectly and injured a consumer
A product that becomes outdated and less safe than newer models
A product that the consumer modified before use in an unauthorized way
questions.answer: A manufacturing defect where one unit was assembled incorrectly and injured a consumer
questions.explanation: A manufacturing defect occurs when a specific unit deviates from the intended design and causes injury — classic strict liability scenario where the manufacturer is liable even without negligence.
